lines.line: # Introduction and Purpose of the Interview

> Erik Hornung introduces himself, the context of the interview, and the America 250 in Idaho Commemoration project. He explains the goals of the oral history, the focus on rodeo, and the structure of the interview. The introduction sets a relaxed tone and outlines the process for the conversation with Brady Portner.

metadata.title: Introduction and Purpose of the Interview
metadata.synopsis: Erik Hornung introduces himself, the context of the interview, and the America 250 in Idaho Commemoration project. He explains the goals of the oral history, the focus on rodeo, and the structure of the interview. The introduction sets a relaxed tone and outlines the process for the conversation with Brady Portner.

lines.line: # Brady Portner’s Background and Family Heritage

> Brady Portner shares details about his upbringing in the Treasure Valley, his family's deep roots in cowboy culture, and the generational legacy of raising horses and cattle. He describes how both sides of his family were involved in rodeo and livestock, and how these influences shaped his early aspirations. Brady also mentions his initial experiences with riding and roping, and his natural inclination toward bull riding.

metadata.title: Brady Portner’s Background and Family Heritage
metadata.synopsis: Brady Portner shares details about his upbringing in the Treasure Valley, his family's deep roots in cowboy culture, and the generational legacy of raising horses and cattle. He describes how both sides of his family were involved in rodeo and livestock, and how these influences shaped his early aspirations. Brady also mentions his initial experiences with riding and roping, and his natural inclination toward bull riding.

lines.line: # Family Influence and Early Rodeo Experiences

> Brady discusses his father's and uncle's careers as bull riders, including his father's achievements in the Idaho Cowboys Association and participation in pro rodeos. He reflects on traveling to some rodeos as a child, his father's retirement from bull riding, and the family's focus on supporting the children in junior rodeo. The conversation highlights the supportive but non-pushy environment that encouraged Brady's interest in rodeo.

metadata.title: Family Influence and Early Rodeo Experiences
metadata.synopsis: Brady discusses his father's and uncle's careers as bull riders, including his father's achievements in the Idaho Cowboys Association and participation in pro rodeos. He reflects on traveling to some rodeos as a child, his father's retirement from bull riding, and the family's focus on supporting the children in junior rodeo. The conversation highlights the supportive but non-pushy environment that encouraged Brady's interest in rodeo.

lines.line: # Memories, Camaraderie, and the Rodeo Lifestyle

> Brady describes his favorite aspect of rodeo as the memories and camaraderie formed while traveling and competing. He emphasizes the unique lifestyle of being on the road, the bonds with fellow competitors, and the experiences that go beyond the arena. The discussion draws parallels to other sports, highlighting the importance of relationships and shared adventures.

metadata.title: Memories, Camaraderie, and the Rodeo Lifestyle
metadata.synopsis: Brady describes his favorite aspect of rodeo as the memories and camaraderie formed while traveling and competing. He emphasizes the unique lifestyle of being on the road, the bonds with fellow competitors, and the experiences that go beyond the arena. The discussion draws parallels to other sports, highlighting the importance of relationships and shared adventures.

lines.line: # Caldwell Night Rodeo and Local Rodeo Culture

> The conversation shifts to the significance of the Caldwell Night Rodeo, its prestige, and its role as a major event in the rodeo world. Brady explains the differences between local and pro rodeos, the high caliber of competitors and livestock at Caldwell, and his personal connection to the event. He compares Caldwell to other notable rodeos and discusses the pride of having such a prestigious event in a small community.

metadata.title: Caldwell Night Rodeo and Local Rodeo Culture
metadata.synopsis: The conversation shifts to the significance of the Caldwell Night Rodeo, its prestige, and its role as a major event in the rodeo world. Brady explains the differences between local and pro rodeos, the high caliber of competitors and livestock at Caldwell, and his personal connection to the event. He compares Caldwell to other notable rodeos and discusses the pride of having such a prestigious event in a small community.

lines.line: # Competing, Achievements, and the Business of Rodeo

> Brady talks about his recent competitions, best finishes, and the rewards associated with winning at major rodeos like Caldwell. He explains the financial aspects of rodeo, including entry fees, travel expenses, and the role of sponsorships. The discussion covers the realities of making a living as a professional rodeo cowboy and the variability in support among competitors.

metadata.title: Competing, Achievements, and the Business of Rodeo
metadata.synopsis: Brady talks about his recent competitions, best finishes, and the rewards associated with winning at major rodeos like Caldwell. He explains the financial aspects of rodeo, including entry fees, travel expenses, and the role of sponsorships. The discussion covers the realities of making a living as a professional rodeo cowboy and the variability in support among competitors.

lines.line: # Making It as a Professional Bull Rider

> Brady reflects on the moment he realized he had become a professional bull rider, describing the transition to full-time competition with his traveling partner. He explains the qualifications required to enter top-tier rodeos and the sense of accomplishment that comes with reaching that level. The conversation highlights the dedication and hard work needed to succeed in professional rodeo.

metadata.title: Making It as a Professional Bull Rider
metadata.synopsis: Brady reflects on the moment he realized he had become a professional bull rider, describing the transition to full-time competition with his traveling partner. He explains the qualifications required to enter top-tier rodeos and the sense of accomplishment that comes with reaching that level. The conversation highlights the dedication and hard work needed to succeed in professional rodeo.

lines.line: # Training and Preparation for Bull Riding

> Brady discusses the various methods bull riders use to train, including physical exercise, riding horses for balance, and practicing on stationary barrels. He shares his own approach to preparation and describes his first experience riding a full-sized bull at age 12. The conversation touches on the importance of hands-on experience and the progression from calves and steers to larger bulls.

metadata.title: Training and Preparation for Bull Riding
metadata.synopsis: Brady discusses the various methods bull riders use to train, including physical exercise, riding horses for balance, and practicing on stationary barrels. He shares his own approach to preparation and describes his first experience riding a full-sized bull at age 12. The conversation touches on the importance of hands-on experience and the progression from calves and steers to larger bulls.

lines.line: # Fear, Respect, and Memorable Rides

> Brady addresses the emotional aspects of bull riding, emphasizing respect for the animals rather than fear. He describes the excitement and adrenaline of a successful ride, recalling his most memorable performance in Cheyenne where he won with a high-scoring ride. The discussion highlights the thrill of competition and the satisfaction of mastering challenging bulls.

metadata.title: Fear, Respect, and Memorable Rides
metadata.synopsis: Brady addresses the emotional aspects of bull riding, emphasizing respect for the animals rather than fear. He describes the excitement and adrenaline of a successful ride, recalling his most memorable performance in Cheyenne where he won with a high-scoring ride. The discussion highlights the thrill of competition and the satisfaction of mastering challenging bulls.

lines.line: # Rodeo Season, Motivation, and Passion for the Sport

> Brady explains the structure of the rodeo season, the importance of passion and intrinsic motivation, and the financial and emotional rewards of competing. He discusses the necessity of loving the sport for its own sake, as external motivations alone are not enough to sustain a career in bull riding. The conversation explores the balance between personal fulfillment and professional success.

metadata.title: Rodeo Season, Motivation, and Passion for the Sport
metadata.synopsis: Brady explains the structure of the rodeo season, the importance of passion and intrinsic motivation, and the financial and emotional rewards of competing. He discusses the necessity of loving the sport for its own sake, as external motivations alone are not enough to sustain a career in bull riding. The conversation explores the balance between personal fulfillment and professional success.

lines.line: # Injuries, Recovery, and Mentality Toward Risk

> Brady recounts his experience with a head injury at Cheyenne, the process of recovery, and the culture of toughness in bull riding. He describes the acceptance of risk as part of the sport, the quick return to competition after injuries, and the stories of friends who have endured serious setbacks. The discussion underscores the resilience and determination required to continue riding despite physical dangers.

metadata.title: Injuries, Recovery, and Mentality Toward Risk
metadata.synopsis: Brady recounts his experience with a head injury at Cheyenne, the process of recovery, and the culture of toughness in bull riding. He describes the acceptance of risk as part of the sport, the quick return to competition after injuries, and the stories of friends who have endured serious setbacks. The discussion underscores the resilience and determination required to continue riding despite physical dangers.

lines.line: # The Cowboy Mindset and Toughness

> Brady and the interviewer discuss the unique toughness and mentality required in bull riding and cowboy culture. They compare the resilience of rodeo athletes to other sports and professions, emphasizing the values of hard work, determination, and self-reliance. Brady expresses pride in his identity as a cowboy and the tradition of overcoming adversity for the sake of the lifestyle and community.

lines.line: # The Role of Family and Fatherhood

> Brady reflects on how becoming a father influences his perspective on bull riding and responsibility. He discusses the desire to share his lifestyle with his son, the importance of not pushing children into the sport, and the realities of risk in modern bull riding. The conversation explores the balance between legacy, personal choice, and preparing the next generation for the challenges of rodeo.

lines.line: # Horse Training and Life Beyond Bull Riding

> Brady explains the distinction between bull riding and bullfighting, then describes his parallel career as a horse trainer. He shares how he developed a passion for training horses, the skills he learned from working with other trainers, and the flexibility and fulfillment this work provides. Brady discusses the integration of family life with his horse training business and the opportunities it creates for his future.

He shares how he developed a passion for training horses, the skills he learned from working with other trainers, and the flexibility and fulfillment this work provides. Brady discusses the integration of family life with his horse training business and the opportunities it creates for his future.

lines.line: # Financial Aspects and Career Planning

> Brady talks about the financial realities of rodeo, the importance of having a secondary source of income, and the transition from relying solely on bull riding to building a horse training business. He reflects on career longevity, the typical retirement age for bull riders, and his own plans for returning to competition. The conversation highlights the value of planning for life after rodeo and the benefits of diversifying skills.

lines.line: # Changes in Rodeo and Hopes for the Future

> Brady discusses the evolution of rodeo, including technological advancements, increased prize money, and the professionalization of local events. He shares his hopes for the future, emphasizing the importance of maintaining free will for competitors, increasing financial rewards, and preserving the unique mentality of rodeo athletes. Brady expresses admiration for the dedication of rodeo cowboys and the need for greater recognition and support from the public.

lines.line: # Rodeo Idols and Final Reflections

> Brady names JB Mooney as his rodeo idol and shares a story about meeting him, highlighting the authenticity and generosity of his hero. He reflects on the importance of role models, the fulfillment of achieving his childhood dreams, and his gratitude for the cowboy lifestyle. The interview concludes with Brady expressing appreciation for the opportunity to share his experiences and the significance of his work in the broader context of American life.

children.text: # Introduction and Purpose of the Interview

> Erik Hornung introduces himself, the context of the interview, and the America 250 in Idaho Commemoration project. He explains the goals of the oral history, the focus on rodeo, and the structure of the interview. The introduction sets a relaxed tone and outlines the process for the conversation with Brady Portner.
# Brady Portner’s Background and Family Heritage

> Brady Portner shares details about his upbringing in the Treasure Valley, his family's deep roots in cowboy culture, and the generational legacy of raising horses and cattle. He describes how both sides of his family were involved in rodeo and livestock, and how these influences shaped his early aspirations. Brady also mentions his initial experiences with riding and roping, and his natural inclination toward bull riding.
# Family Influence and Early Rodeo Experiences

> Brady discusses his father's and uncle's careers as bull riders, including his father's achievements in the Idaho Cowboys Association and participation in pro rodeos. He reflects on traveling to some rodeos as a child, his father's retirement from bull riding, and the family's focus on supporting the children in junior rodeo. The conversation highlights the supportive but non-pushy environment that encouraged Brady's interest in rodeo.
# Memories, Camaraderie, and the Rodeo Lifestyle

> Brady describes his favorite aspect of rodeo as the memories and camaraderie formed while traveling and competing. He emphasizes the unique lifestyle of being on the road, the bonds with fellow competitors, and the experiences that go beyond the arena. The discussion draws parallels to other sports, highlighting the importance of relationships and shared adventures.
# Caldwell Night Rodeo and Local Rodeo Culture

> The conversation shifts to the significance of the Caldwell Night Rodeo, its prestige, and its role as a major event in the rodeo world. Brady explains the differences between local and pro rodeos, the high caliber of competitors and livestock at Caldwell, and his personal connection to the event. He compares Caldwell to other notable rodeos and discusses the pride of having such a prestigious event in a small community.
# Competing, Achievements, and the Business of Rodeo

> Brady talks about his recent competitions, best finishes, and the rewards associated with winning at major rodeos like Caldwell. He explains the financial aspects of rodeo, including entry fees, travel expenses, and the role of sponsorships. The discussion covers the realities of making a living as a professional rodeo cowboy and the variability in support among competitors.
# Making It as a Professional Bull Rider

> Brady reflects on the moment he realized he had become a professional bull rider, describing the transition to full-time competition with his traveling partner. He explains the qualifications required to enter top-tier rodeos and the sense of accomplishment that comes with reaching that level. The conversation highlights the dedication and hard work needed to succeed in professional rodeo.
# Training and Preparation for Bull Riding

> Brady discusses the various methods bull riders use to train, including physical exercise, riding horses for balance, and practicing on stationary barrels. He shares his own approach to preparation and describes his first experience riding a full-sized bull at age 12. The conversation touches on the importance of hands-on experience and the progression from calves and steers to larger bulls.
# Fear, Respect, and Memorable Rides

> Brady addresses the emotional aspects of bull riding, emphasizing respect for the animals rather than fear. He describes the excitement and adrenaline of a successful ride, recalling his most memorable performance in Cheyenne where he won with a high-scoring ride. The discussion highlights the thrill of competition and the satisfaction of mastering challenging bulls.
# Rodeo Season, Motivation, and Passion for the Sport

> Brady explains the structure of the rodeo season, the importance of passion and intrinsic motivation, and the financial and emotional rewards of competing. He discusses the necessity of loving the sport for its own sake, as external motivations alone are not enough to sustain a career in bull riding. The conversation explores the balance between personal fulfillment and professional success.
# Injuries, Recovery, and Mentality Toward Risk

> Brady recounts his experience with a head injury at Cheyenne, the process of recovery, and the culture of toughness in bull riding. He describes the acceptance of risk as part of the sport, the quick return to competition after injuries, and the stories of friends who have endured serious setbacks. The discussion underscores the resilience and determination required to continue riding despite physical dangers.
# The Cowboy Mindset and Toughness

> Brady and the interviewer discuss the unique toughness and mentality required in bull riding and cowboy culture. They compare the resilience of rodeo athletes to other sports and professions, emphasizing the values of hard work, determination, and self-reliance. Brady expresses pride in his identity as a cowboy and the tradition of overcoming adversity for the sake of the lifestyle and community.
# The Role of Family and Fatherhood

> Brady reflects on how becoming a father influences his perspective on bull riding and responsibility. He discusses the desire to share his lifestyle with his son, the importance of not pushing children into the sport, and the realities of risk in modern bull riding. The conversation explores the balance between legacy, personal choice, and preparing the next generation for the challenges of rodeo.
# Horse Training and Life Beyond Bull Riding

> Brady explains the distinction between bull riding and bullfighting, then describes his parallel career as a horse trainer. He shares how he developed a passion for training horses, the skills he learned from working with other trainers, and the flexibility and fulfillment this work provides. Brady discusses the integration of family life with his horse training business and the opportunities it creates for his future.
# Financial Aspects and Career Planning

> Brady talks about the financial realities of rodeo, the importance of having a secondary source of income, and the transition from relying solely on bull riding to building a horse training business. He reflects on career longevity, the typical retirement age for bull riders, and his own plans for returning to competition. The conversation highlights the value of planning for life after rodeo and the benefits of diversifying skills.
# Changes in Rodeo and Hopes for the Future

> Brady discusses the evolution of rodeo, including technological advancements, increased prize money, and the professionalization of local events. He shares his hopes for the future, emphasizing the importance of maintaining free will for competitors, increasing financial rewards, and preserving the unique mentality of rodeo athletes. Brady expresses admiration for the dedication of rodeo cowboys and the need for greater recognition and support from the public.
# Rodeo Idols and Final Reflections

> Brady names JB Mooney as his rodeo idol and shares a story about meeting him, highlighting the authenticity and generosity of his hero. He reflects on the importance of role models, the fulfillment of achieving his childhood dreams, and his gratitude for the cowboy lifestyle. The interview concludes with Brady expressing appreciation for the opportunity to share his experiences and the significance of his work in the broader context of American life.

story.title: Interview with Brady Portenier in his Leather Work Shed
story.description: The interview with Brady Portner, a rodeo cowboy and horse trainer, explores his life and career in the rodeo world. Brady, a fourth or fifth-generation cowboy, discusses his upbringing in Treasure Valley and his natural pathway into rodeo due to family influence, particularly his father, a former bull rider. Emphasizing camaraderie, Brady shares how rodeo offers a unique lifestyle of travel and community. Despite experiencing significant injuries, including a head injury in Cheyenne, his passion for bull riding remains undeterred. Brady also talks about his horse training business, a primary income source during a break from professional rodeo. He reflects on the evolution of rodeo, advocating for preserving the cowboy spirit and increasing financial opportunities for new generations.
